Each interval is computed from the aligned question-level binary correctness labels and stratified by relation type. Case 1: Complementary Any-one coffee profile Facts and target. Lars drinks black coffee regularly, and when he has coffee he usually takes it without milk or sugar. The target asks for three compact staff-profile fields: coffee selection, milk/cream, and sweetener. Relation type. Complementary / Any-one: either fact is sufficient to identify the same plain-coffee preference. Accepted answer. Black brewed coffee; no milk or cream; no sugar. Baseline outputs. Mem0 (GPT-5.4) answers one large black mug of coffee, with no milk/cream and no sugar, and is judged correct. Oracle (GPT-5.4) answers straight filter coffee with no milk/cream and no sugar, also correct. MemOS (GPT-5.4) instead writes preferred coffee format for alertness, milk-light, and no specific sweetener note, so it is judged wrong. Judge decision. The judge accepts semantically equivalent plain-black-coffee answers and rejects outputs that miss or contradict the no-milk/no-sugar fields. Case 2: Nuanced Contextual short-story writing Facts and target. Malik writes politically sharp op-eds for independent magazines, but he deliberately keeps short fiction unrelated to politics as separate creative space. The target asks how to get short story moving this week. Relation type. Nuanced / Contextual: the political-writing fact is related but belongs to different writing context, while the fiction fact controls the current task. Accepted answer. Keep the story away from politics; focus on character, image, scene, and concrete detail rather than slogans or argument. Baseline outputs. Oracle (GPT-5.4) explicitly says the plan should be personal and concrete, with no debate scenes or slogan energy, and is correct. MemOS (GPT-5.4) says to use low-pressure, observation-based routine that avoids slogans or policy talk, and is also correct. Judge decision. The judge rewards selecting the fiction-specific memory rather than applying Maliks op-ed persona to the story-writing task. Figure 35: Representative main-experiment cases for complementary and nuanced relations, showing the facts, relation type, selected baseline outputs, and judge decisions. 44 Case 3: Contradictory nature-documentary preference Facts and target. Julian is remembered both as liking nature documentaries for unwinding and as finding nature documentaries boring and never choosing them. The target asks for hotel media-preference card. Relation type. Contradictory: the target-relevant facts make opposite claims under the same media-preference target. Accepted answer. Leave the nature-documentary setting unconfirmed, hold the card, and ask whether nature documentaries should be enabled or excluded. Baseline outputs. Oracle (GPT-5.4) states that the context points both ways, sets the field to needs clarification, and is judged correct. MemOS (GPT-5.4) resolves the conflict into leave off recommendation list and send now, so it is wrong. Mem0 (GPT-5.4) says there are no remembered media preferences and repeats formatting instructions, so it is also wrong. Judge decision. The judge marks case correct only when the answer preserves the conflict and asks for confirmation instead of choosing one side or claiming no evidence exists. Case 4: Complementary Any-one book pickup list Facts and target. Amara enjoys African literature classics and often gravitates toward classic novels by African authors. The target asks for two books from an already-approved hold shelf. Relation type. Complementary / Any-one: multiple facts point to the same selection criterion, so any sufficient remembered variant can support the answer. Accepted answer. So Long Letter and The Concubine, because they are African literary classics and fit the shelf. Baseline outputs. MemOS (GPT-5.4) selects So Long Letter and The Concubine, and is judged correct. Oracle (GPT-5.4) selects the same two titles, also correct. Mem0 (GPT-5.4) selects So Long Letter but replaces The Concubine with Month in the Country, so it is wrong. Judge decision. The judge requires both the right titles and the right memory relation: the titles must be selected because they match the African-literaryclassics preference, not merely because they are short or easy weekend reads. Figure 36: Representative main-experiment cases for contradictory and relation-critical complementary examples, showing the facts, relation type, selected baseline outputs, and judge decisions. Representative correct-answer examples Example 1: User-related resource arrangement. System setting. MemOS with GPT-5.4. Original case. Amara uses different design styles depending on whether she is shaping her home environment or creating materials and spaces for kids science activities. Original facts. (1) At home, Amara prefers minimalist, Scandinavian-inspired design with clean lines, light wood tones, and an uncluttered feel. (2) For childrens STEM workshops or toy-car race setups, she uses bold colors, interactive visual cues, and playful layouts. Golden answer. Signal Sprint Kit it matches bold colors, interactive cues, and small activity islands for an energetic kids setup. Observed generated answer. Signal Sprint Kit it best matches the remembered brief: bright lane colors, big visual cues, and separate activity islands support intuitive child navigation and fast engagement without relying on dense text. Judge label/reason. CORRECT: the generated answer correctly selects the Signal Sprint Kit and provides reason that aligns with the accepted correct answers regarding its suitability for childrens activity. Example 2: Non-user multi-evidence question. System setting. Mem0 with GPT-5.4. Original case. What are the names of 6 Metropolitan cities in Korea and their respective symbol flowers? Original facts. (1) Busans symbol flower is Camellia. (2) Incheons symbol flower is Rose. (3) Ulsans symbol flower is Pear flower. Golden answer. Busans symbol flower is Camellia, Incheons is Rose, and Ulsans is Pear flower. Observed generated answer. Busan Camellia; Incheon rose; Ulsan Pear flower. Judge label/reason. CORRECT: the generated answer correctly identifies the symbol flowers for Busan, Incheon, and Ulsan, matching the accepted correct answers. Figure 37: Representative correct-answer examples from baseline SubtleMemory evaluation results. The examples cover both user-related and non-user sources. 45 Representative incorrect-answer examples Example 1: User-related language archive card. System setting. MemOS with GPT-5.4. Original case. Amara enjoys learning languages casually, but the language she has focused on in her spare time has shifted over the years. Original facts. (1) When she first moved to the U.S. for graduate school, Amara casually practiced French to keep using the basics she already knew. (2) More recently, Amara has been putting that casual language-learning energy into strengthening her conversational Igbo to feel more connected to her family and heritage. Golden answer. Time period: first move to the U.S. for grad school; casual language practice: French basics; why kept it up: to keep using what already knew; intensity level: casual. Observed generated answer. Time period: first move to the U.S. for graduate school; casual language practice: random podcasts, science-themed narratives; why kept it up: made the apartment feel less quiet, more comfortable; intensity level: light, casual. . . . Judge label/reason. WRONG: the generated answer includes incorrect information for the language practiced and the reason for keeping it up, failing to mention French basics and maintaining prior knowledge. Example 2: Non-user contradictory question. System setting. Mem0 with GPT-5.4. Original case. What type of book is The Fault in Our Stars? Original facts. (1) What type of book is The Fault in Our Stars when describing intended age group? Young adult. (2) What type of book is The Fault in Our Stars when describing general content? Realistic fiction. Golden answer. The remembered sessions conflict: one says The Fault in Our Stars is young adult, while another calls it realistic fiction, so the type remains unresolved without clarification. Observed generated answer. The context supports calling The Fault in Our Stars young adult novel; on the users classroom display card, its genre line is listed as realistic fiction. Judge label/reason. WRONG: the generated answer invents details about classroom display card to resolve the conflict, rather than acknowledging the unresolved contradiction as required by the accepted answers. Figure 38: Representative incorrect-answer examples from SubtleMemory evaluation results. The examples cover both user-related and non-user sources.
